articles.headline: India-EU FTA Negotiations See Major Breakthrough on Key Sectors
articles.explainer:
• After years of intense negotiations, India and the European Union announced significant progress on a Free Trade Agreement (FTA), particularly in critical sectors like digital trade and sustainable development.
• The joint statement highlighted consensus on intellectual property rights and investment facilitation, paving the way for a comprehensive deal by year-end.
• This FTA aims to boost bilateral trade significantly, potentially increasing it by over 40% in five years, and enhancing strategic cooperation.
• Key sticking points like market access for automobiles and agricultural products are still under discussion but show signs of compromise.
• Experts believe the deal will diversify India's export basket and provide EU businesses greater access to the Indian market, strengthening global supply chains.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests
prelimsPoints: India-EU FTA aims to boost bilateral trade and strategic cooperation.
Key areas of breakthrough: digital trade, sustainable development, IPR, investment facilitation.
European Union (EU) is a major trading partner for India.
FTA negotiations resumed in 2021 after a pause since 2013.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic and economic implications of the India-EU FTA for India's foreign policy and economic growth.
Discuss the challenges and opportunities for Indian industries arising from increased competition and market access.
Evaluate how this FTA aligns with India's broader trade policy objectives and its 'Act East' and 'Look West' strategies.
Examine the role of sustainable development and digital trade clauses in modern FTAs.
articles.imageDescription: Flags of India and the European Union displayed together, symbolizing strong bilateral ties and a potential trade agreement.
articles.trivia: The EU is India's third-largest trading partner, accounting for 10.8% of India's total trade in goods in 2022-23.

articles.headline: Supreme Court Upholds State's Power to Levy Environmental Compensation on Polluting Industries
articles.explainer:
• The Supreme Court, in a landmark judgment, affirmed the power of state pollution control boards to impose environmental compensation on industries violating pollution norms.
• The ruling came in a case challenging a state government's notification imposing fines based on the 'Polluter Pays Principle' and the 'Precautionary Principle'.
• The court emphasized that environmental protection is a constitutional mandate under Article 48A and 51A(g), and states have inherent power to ensure ecological balance.
• This judgment is expected to strengthen environmental governance and act as a deterrent against industrial pollution across the country.
• It clarifies the legal framework for calculating and imposing compensation, directing for transparent and scientific methodologies.
gsSubTopics: Structure, organization and functioning of the Executive and the Judiciary
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
Environmental pollution and degradation
prelimsPoints: Supreme Court affirmed state's power to levy environmental compensation.
Judgment based on 'Polluter Pays Principle' and 'Precautionary Principle'.
Constitutional provisions for environment: Article 48A (DPSP) and Article 51A(g) (Fundamental Duty).
State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs) are statutory bodies under Water Act, 1974 and Air Act, 1981.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of the 'Polluter Pays Principle' and 'Precautionary Principle' in India's environmental jurisprudence.
Analyze how this Supreme Court ruling strengthens federal environmental governance and promotes sustainable industrial practices.
Critically evaluate the challenges in implementing environmental compensation mechanisms effectively and ensuring their proper utilization.
Examine the role of the judiciary in upholding environmental rights and balancing industrial development with ecological protection.
articles.imageDescription: A gavel resting on law books, with a blurred background of a factory emitting smoke, symbolizing legal action against pollution.
articles.trivia: The 'Polluter Pays Principle' was first introduced in India in the Vellore Citizens' Welfare Forum v. Union of India case (1996).

articles.headline: ISRO Successfully Conducts Critical Test of Reusable Launch Vehicle (RLV) Prototype
articles.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) announced the successful completion of a crucial test flight for its Reusable Launch Vehicle (RLV) prototype, 'Pushpak'.
• The test involved a precise landing experiment, simulating conditions for future orbital re-entry and autonomous landing on a runway.
• This achievement marks a significant step towards developing a fully reusable two-stage orbital launch vehicle, aiming to drastically reduce space launch costs.
• The RLV-TD (Technology Demonstrator) project is a long-term vision of ISRO to make space access more affordable and sustainable.
• Future tests will focus on orbital flight and re-entry characteristics, integrating advanced materials and propulsion systems.
gsSubTopics: Achievements of Indians in science & technology
Indigenization of technology and developing new technology
Awareness in the fields of Space
prelimsPoints: ISRO's RLV project aims to reduce space launch costs.
The prototype is named 'Pushpak'.

mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic importance of Reusable Launch Vehicle technology for India's space program and its global competitiveness.
Analyze the economic benefits and environmental advantages of developing reusable launch systems compared to traditional expendable rockets.
Examine the challenges and technological advancements required to achieve a fully operational reusable launch vehicle.
Evaluate India's position in the global space race with indigenous innovations like the RLV.
articles.imageDescription: An artistic rendition of ISRO's 'Pushpak' Reusable Launch Vehicle prototype descending for a landing on a runway.
articles.trivia: ISRO first successfully conducted the RLV-TD HEX (Hypersonic Flight Experiment) in 2016, demonstrating its controlled flight and re-entry capability.

articles.headline: National Skill Mission to Prioritize 'Green Jobs' Training for Rural Youth
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Skill Development and Entrepreneurship announced a major overhaul of the National Skill Mission, with a dedicated focus on training rural youth for 'green jobs'.
• This initiative aims to align India's workforce with the growing demands of renewable energy, sustainable agriculture, waste management, and eco-tourism sectors.
• The program will involve collaborations with industry partners, NGOs, and local self-help groups to ensure relevant and demand-driven training modules.
• The move is expected to address rural unemployment, reduce migration to urban areas, and contribute to India's climate action goals.
• Pilot projects have shown promising results in states like Rajasthan and Gujarat, leading to the nationwide expansion.

mainsPoints: Analyze how the focus on 'green jobs' in the National Skill Mission can address both unemployment and climate change challenges in India.
Discuss the potential benefits and challenges of integrating green skills into existing vocational training frameworks, especially in rural areas.

Examine the socio-economic impact of promoting green entrepreneurship among rural youth.
articles.imageDescription: Young people in a vocational training center learning about solar panel installation, representing 'green jobs' training.
articles.trivia: According to the International Labour Organization (ILO), 'green jobs' are decent jobs that contribute to preserving or restoring the environment.

articles.headline: India Calls for Review of Indus Water Treaty Provisions Amid Changing Hydrological Realities
articles.explainer:
• India has reiterated its call for a review of certain provisions of the Indus Water Treaty (IWT) with Pakistan, citing changing hydrological realities and climate change impacts.
• The Ministry of External Affairs emphasized the need for a more equitable and efficient utilization of shared water resources, in light of reduced glacier melt and altered river flows.
• Pakistan, however, has maintained its stance on strict adherence to the existing treaty, which largely allocates western rivers to Pakistan and eastern rivers to India.
• India has been undertaking several hydroelectric projects on its allocated rivers, which Pakistan often views as potential violations, leading to ongoing disputes.
• The World Bank, a guarantor of the treaty, is being kept informed of the developments, as both nations seek a diplomatic resolution.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
prelimsPoints: India seeks review of Indus Water Treaty (IWT) provisions.
Reasons cited: changing hydrological realities and climate change impacts.
IWT allocates Western Rivers (Indus, Jhelum, Chenab) to Pakistan, Eastern Rivers (Ravi, Beas, Sutlej) to India.
The World Bank is a signatory/guarantor of the IWT (signed 1960).
mainsPoints: Analyze the historical context and key provisions of the Indus Water Treaty and its significance for India-Pakistan relations.
Discuss the implications of climate change on shared water resources and the challenges it poses for existing water-sharing agreements.
Evaluate India's arguments for reviewing the IWT and the potential diplomatic and strategic ramifications of such a move.
Examine the role of international mediators like the World Bank in resolving water disputes between riparian states.
articles.imageDescription: A map showing the Indus River system flowing through India and Pakistan, with national flags superimposed.
articles.trivia: The Indus Water Treaty is considered one of the most successful water-sharing treaties in the world, having survived multiple conflicts between India and Pakistan.

articles.headline: Government Reviews Implementation of New Data Protection Framework, Addresses Initial Challenges
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) conducted a comprehensive review of the initial implementation phase of India's new Data Protection Framework.
• The framework, which came into effect earlier this year, aims to regulate the processing of personal data and protect citizens' privacy rights.
• Key challenges identified include ensuring compliance by smaller entities, clarity on cross-border data flows, and establishing the Data Protection Board effectively.
• The government reassured stakeholders of its commitment to a robust and adaptable framework, promising clarifications and amendments where necessary.
• The framework is critical for fostering trust in India's digital economy and aligning with global data governance standards.

articles.trivia: The Puttaswamy judgment (2017) by the Supreme Court declared privacy a fundamental right under Article 21 of the Indian Constitution, paving the way for data protection legislation.

articles.headline: RBI's Monetary Policy Committee Expresses Concern Over Persistent Food Inflation
articles.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India's (RBI) Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) concluded its bi-monthly meeting, maintaining the repo rate but expressing significant concern over persistent food inflation.
• While headline inflation has shown signs of moderation, volatile food prices, especially for vegetables and pulses, continue to pose a challenge to the inflation target.
• The MPC reiterated its commitment to bringing inflation within the target band of 4% (+/- 2%) while supporting growth.
• Analysts anticipate continued vigilance from the RBI, with future policy decisions heavily dependent on monsoon performance and global commodity prices.
• The committee emphasized the need for supply-side interventions by the government to complement monetary policy efforts.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Government Budgeting
Inflation and its impact

mainsPoints: Discuss the primary objectives and instruments of India's monetary policy, particularly in the context of persistent inflation.

Examine the challenges faced by the RBI in balancing inflation control with economic growth objectives.
articles.imageDescription: RBI logo with graphs showing rising inflation, symbolizing the central bank's focus on economic stability.
articles.trivia: The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) was constituted in 2016, making the inflation targeting framework statutory.

articles.headline: DRDO Unveils Advanced Indigenous Anti-Drone System for Border Security
articles.explainer:
• The Defence Research and Development Organisation (DRDO) successfully demonstrated an advanced indigenous anti-drone system designed to counter emerging threats from unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs).
• The system incorporates both 'soft kill' (jamming, spoofing) and 'hard kill' (laser-based interception) capabilities, offering comprehensive protection against rogue drones.
• Developed in collaboration with private sector partners, it aims to enhance border security and protect critical infrastructure from drone-based attacks.
• The system features artificial intelligence for threat detection and classification, with a range of several kilometers.
• This development is a significant boost to India's 'Atmanirbhar Bharat' initiative in the defence sector, reducing reliance on foreign technology.

Indigenization of technology and developing new technology

articles.headline: Ministry of Urban Affairs Releases Guidelines for Mitigating Urban Heat Island Effect
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs released a comprehensive set of guidelines for urban local bodies to mitigate the 'Urban Heat Island' (UHI) effect.
• The guidelines emphasize green infrastructure development, promotion of cool roofs and pavements, and increased tree cover in urban areas.
• UHI effect leads to higher temperatures in cities compared to surrounding rural areas, impacting public health, energy consumption, and air quality.
• Cities are encouraged to develop specific action plans, integrate UHI mitigation into urban planning, and leverage smart city technologies.
• This initiative is part of India's broader climate resilience strategy, aiming to make cities more sustainable and livable.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Disaster and disaster management
Urbanization, their problems and their remedies
prelimsPoints: Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs released guidelines for UHI mitigation.

Mitigation strategies: green infrastructure, cool roofs, increased tree cover.

mainsPoints: Explain the phenomenon of the Urban Heat Island effect and its various impacts on urban environments and public health.
Discuss the effectiveness of different mitigation strategies proposed in the guidelines, such as green infrastructure and cool surfaces.
Analyze the challenges in implementing UHI mitigation measures in rapidly urbanizing Indian cities and suggest policy interventions.
Evaluate the role of urban planning and smart city initiatives in building climate-resilient cities in India.
articles.imageDescription: An aerial view of a city with green spaces and 'cool roofs' contrasted with dense concrete buildings, showing UHI mitigation efforts.
articles.trivia: Cities can be 1-3°C warmer than their surrounding rural areas during the day, and up to 12°C warmer at night, due to the UHI effect.

articles.headline: India Champions Global Health Equity in G20 Health Ministers' Meeting
articles.explainer:
• India played a pivotal role in the G20 Health Ministers' meeting, advocating for global health equity, affordable access to vaccines, and strengthening pandemic preparedness.
• The Indian delegation highlighted its experience in vaccine manufacturing and delivery, offering to share best practices and technological expertise with developing nations.
• Discussions focused on establishing a robust global health architecture, improving health workforce capacity, and leveraging digital health solutions.
• The G20 Health Declaration emphasized collaboration in research and development and equitable distribution of medical countermeasures.
• India's stance aligns with its 'Vasudhaiva Kutumbakam' (The world is one family) philosophy, promoting collective global health security.

Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health

articles.headline: Government Expands Subsidy Scheme for Agricultural Drones to Boost Productivity
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare announced an expansion of its subsidy scheme for purchasing agricultural drones, making the technology more accessible to farmers.
• The scheme provides financial assistance for buying drones and for establishing custom hiring centers for drone services in rural areas.
• Drones are being increasingly used for precision agriculture tasks such as crop health monitoring, pesticide spraying, and nutrient assessment, leading to higher efficiency and reduced input costs.
• The expansion aims to further modernize Indian agriculture, improve farm productivity, and address labor shortages.
• Training programs for farmers and rural entrepreneurs on drone operation and maintenance are also being scaled up.

articles.headline: Archaeologists Unearth Evidence of Ancient Settlement in Haryana, Dating Back to Harappan Period
articles.explainer:
• A team of archaeologists has unearthed significant evidence of an ancient settlement in Haryana, believed to date back to the mature Harappan period (c. 2600–1900 BCE).
• The excavation site revealed pottery, terracotta figurines, beads, and structural remains, indicating a well-developed urban life.
• This discovery adds to the growing understanding of the geographical spread and cultural diversity of the Indus Valley Civilization in the Ghaggar-Hakra basin.
• Further analysis of the artifacts and stratigraphy is expected to provide insights into trade routes, social structures, and daily life of the inhabitants.
• The site's strategic location suggests its potential role as a regional center or a key outpost for resource management.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times
History of India and Indian National Movement
prelimsPoints: Ancient settlement discovered in Haryana, dating to Mature Harappan period.

Adds to understanding of Indus Valley Civilization in Ghaggar-Hakra basin.
Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) is the nodal body.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of recent archaeological discoveries in re-evaluating the geographical extent and characteristics of the Indus Valley Civilization.
Analyze the contribution of such findings to our understanding of urban planning, trade networks, and social organization in ancient India.

Evaluate the role of the Ghaggar-Hakra river system in the development and decline of the Harappan Civilization.
articles.imageDescription: Archaeologists meticulously excavating an ancient site, revealing pottery shards and structural remains.
articles.trivia: Rakhigarhi, also in Haryana, is one of the largest and oldest sites of the Indus Valley Civilization, believed to be larger than Mohenjo-Daro.

articles.headline: Government Invites Tenders for BharatNet Phase III, Aims for Pan-India Rural Connectivity
articles.explainer:
• The Department of Telecommunications (DoT) has initiated the tender process for Phase III of the BharatNet project, aiming to connect all remaining gram panchayats and habitations.
• This phase focuses on leveraging Public-Private Partnership (PPP) models for faster deployment and sustainable operation of optical fiber networks.
• BharatNet is a flagship program to provide broadband connectivity to rural and remote areas, bridging the digital divide.
• Phase III includes provisions for upgrading existing infrastructure and incorporating future-proof technologies like 5G backhaul capabilities.
• The project is crucial for digital inclusion, e-governance, digital education, and healthcare services in rural India.

articles.trivia: BharatNet is one of the world's largest rural broadband connectivity programs, aiming to connect 2.5 lakh gram panchayats.

articles.headline: India Crosses 100 GW Mark in Installed Solar Energy Capacity
articles.explainer:
• India has achieved a significant milestone by crossing 100 GW of installed solar energy capacity, marking a rapid expansion in its renewable energy sector.
• This includes both large-scale solar parks and decentralized rooftop solar installations across the country.
• The accelerated growth is attributed to supportive government policies, declining solar panel costs, and increased private sector investment.
• This achievement brings India closer to its ambitious target of 500 GW of non-fossil fuel energy capacity by 2030.
• The push for solar energy is vital for energy security, reducing carbon emissions, and meeting international climate commitments.

articles.headline: Editorial: The Imperative for a Robust AI Governance Framework in India
articles.explainer:
• This editorial argues for the urgent need for a comprehensive Artificial Intelligence (AI) governance framework in India to manage its rapid adoption.
• It highlights the dual potential of AI for economic growth and societal challenges, including ethical dilemmas, job displacement, and data privacy concerns.
• The piece suggests that India, as a burgeoning digital economy, must proactively develop regulations that foster innovation while ensuring accountability and fairness.
• It calls for a multi-stakeholder approach involving government, industry, academia, and civil society in drafting AI policies.
• The editorial emphasizes learning from global best practices while tailoring solutions to India's unique social and economic context.

articles.headline: Ministry of Education Reviews Progress of National Education Policy (NEP) Implementation
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Education conducted a high-level review meeting to assess the progress and impact of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 across states and institutions.
• Key areas of focus included curricular reforms, teacher training programs, vocational education integration, and the establishment of multidisciplinary institutions.
• While significant progress has been made in certain aspects, challenges remain in achieving universal foundational literacy and numeracy, and ensuring equitable access to quality education.
• The review emphasized the need for greater collaboration between central and state governments, and increased investment in educational infrastructure.
• NEP 2020 aims to transform India's education system to meet the demands of the 21st century and foster holistic development.

articles.headline: Indian Finance Ministry Cautions Against Global Economic Headwinds, Maintains Growth Optimism
articles.explainer:
• The Finance Ministry released its monthly economic review, highlighting continued resilience of the Indian economy but cautioning against escalating global economic headwinds.
• Factors such as geopolitical tensions, persistent inflation in major economies, and tightening global financial conditions pose risks to India's export and investment outlook.
• Despite these external challenges, the Ministry expressed optimism about India's domestic demand, robust public capital expenditure, and strong financial sector.
• Projections for India's GDP growth remain robust, albeit with a watchful approach towards global volatility.
• The report emphasized continued fiscal prudence and structural reforms to sustain long-term growth momentum.

articles.headline: National Mission for Clean Ganga Reviews Progress, Sets New Rejuvenation Targets
articles.explainer:
• The National Mission for Clean Ganga (NMCG) held a high-level meeting to review the progress of various projects under the Namami Gange program and set new targets for river rejuvenation.
• Focus areas include improving municipal wastewater treatment, industrial pollution abatement, and promoting biodiversity conservation along the Ganga basin.
• While significant strides have been made in infrastructure development, challenges persist in community participation and ensuring continuous flow (Aviral Dhara).
• New targets emphasize sustainable river basin management, promotion of natural farming, and protection of riverine ecosystems.
• The mission reiterated its commitment to making Ganga 'Nirmal' (clean) and 'Aviral' (free-flowing) through a holistic approach.

articles.headline: India Advocates for Diversified and Resilient Global Supply Chains at WTO Forum
articles.explainer:
• India, at a World Trade Organization (WTO) forum, strongly advocated for the diversification and resilience of global supply chains to prevent future disruptions.
• The Commerce and Industry Minister highlighted vulnerabilities exposed during recent global crises, emphasizing the need for robust alternative manufacturing hubs.
• India presented its vision for becoming a reliable and competitive partner in global value chains, leveraging its strong manufacturing base and skilled workforce.
• Discussions included proposals for a new WTO framework to facilitate trade in essential goods during emergencies and reduce over-reliance on single sources.
• This stance aligns with India's economic diplomacy to enhance its role in global trade and promote a more equitable international economic order.

articles.headline: India's Startup Ecosystem Continues Robust Growth, Focus on Deep Tech and Rural Innovation
articles.explainer:
• India's startup ecosystem demonstrated continued robust growth in the first half of 2025, with a notable surge in funding for deep tech and rural innovation startups.
• Government initiatives like 'Startup India' and 'Fund of Funds for Startups' have played a crucial role in fostering an entrepreneurial environment.
• The focus is shifting towards sectors like Artificial Intelligence, SpaceTech, Agritech, and MedTech, attracting both domestic and international investors.
• Rural innovation is gaining traction, with startups addressing local challenges in areas like sustainable farming, craft promotion, and last-mile logistics.
• This growth is vital for job creation, economic diversification, and leveraging India's demographic dividend.

articles.headline: Report Highlights Growing Threat of Climate-Induced Migration in Coastal Regions
articles.explainer:
• A new report released by a consortium of environmental NGOs and research institutions warned of the escalating threat of climate-induced migration in India's coastal regions.
• Rising sea levels, increased frequency of extreme weather events, and saltwater intrusion are displacing communities and impacting livelihoods.
• The report projects that millions could be internally displaced by 2050 if adequate adaptation and mitigation measures are not implemented.
• It calls for a national policy framework to address climate migration, including resettlement programs, livelihood diversification, and infrastructure protection.
• The study emphasizes the need for integrating climate resilience into regional development plans and urban planning.

prelimsMocks.question:
With reference to the recent Supreme Court judgment on environmental compensation, consider the following statements:
1. The judgment affirms the power of state pollution control boards to impose compensation based on the 'Polluter Pays Principle'.
2. The ruling explicitly cites Article 48A and Article 51A(g) of the Indian Constitution as foundational for environmental protection.
3. The 'Precautionary Principle' was also a key consideration in the court's decision.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: All three statements are correct. The Supreme Court's judgment upheld the power of state pollution control boards to levy environmental compensation, based on both the 'Polluter Pays Principle' and the 'Precautionary Principle'. It also emphasized that environmental protection is a constitutional mandate under Article 48A (Directive Principles of State Policy) and Article 51A(g) (Fundamental Duties).

prelimsMocks.question: Which of the following statements correctly describes ISRO's 'Pushpak' program?
options: (a) It is a mission to study the Sun's outer corona.
(b) It is a prototype for a fully reusable launch vehicle.
(c) It is a project to develop indigenous cryogenic engines.
(d) It is India's first manned space mission.
prelimsMocks.answer: (b) It is a prototype for a fully reusable launch vehicle.
prelimsMocks.explanation: ISRO's 'Pushpak' is the name given to the Reusable Launch Vehicle (RLV) Technology Demonstrator (RLV-TD) project. Its primary aim is to develop technologies for a fully reusable two-stage orbital launch vehicle, which can significantly reduce the cost of space launches. Aditya-L1 is for studying the Sun, Gaganyaan is for manned space missions, and cryogenic engine development is a separate but related area.

prelimsMocks.question:
Consider the following pairs regarding government initiatives and their objectives:
1. National Skill Mission: Promoting green jobs for rural youth
2. BharatNet Phase III: Providing 5G connectivity to urban centers
3. Namami Gange Programme: Ensuring 'Nirmal' and 'Aviral' flow of river Ganga
Which of the pairs given above is/are correctly matched?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Pair 1 is correctly matched, as the National Skill Mission is prioritizing 'green jobs' training. Pair 3 is correctly matched, as Namami Gange aims for a clean ('Nirmal') and free-flowing ('Aviral') Ganga. Pair 2 is incorrectly matched; BharatNet Phase III aims to connect all remaining gram panchayats and habitations with broadband, primarily optical fiber, not specifically 5G connectivity to urban centers, though it may incorporate 5G backhaul capabilities.

prelimsMocks.question: With respect to the Indus Water Treaty (IWT), which of the following rivers are classified as 'Western Rivers' and primarily allocated to Pakistan?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Under the Indus Water Treaty (IWT), the Eastern Rivers (Ravi, Beas, and Sutlej) are allocated for India's unrestricted use, while the Western Rivers (Indus, Jhelum, and Chenab) are largely allocated to Pakistan, though India has limited rights for non-consumptive uses like power generation.

prelimsMocks.question: Which of the following constitutional provisions are directly related to environmental protection in India?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Article 48A (Directive Principles of State Policy) states that the State shall endeavour to protect and improve the environment and to safeguard the forests and wildlife of the country. Article 51A(g) (Fundamental Duties) states that it shall be the duty of every citizen of India to protect and improve the natural environment including forests, lakes, rivers and wildlife, and to have compassion for living creatures. While other articles like Article 21 (Right to life) have been interpreted by courts to include the right to a clean environment, 48A and 51A(g) are directly and explicitly related to environmental protection.

GS1.headline: Archaeologists Unearth Evidence of Ancient Settlement in Haryana, Dating Back to Harappan Period
GS1.explainer:
• A team of archaeologists has unearthed significant evidence of an ancient settlement in Haryana, believed to date back to the mature Harappan period (c. 2600–1900 BCE).
• The excavation site revealed pottery, terracotta figurines, beads, and structural remains, indicating a well-developed urban life.
• This discovery adds to the growing understanding of the geographical spread and cultural diversity of the Indus Valley Civilization in the Ghaggar-Hakra basin.
• Further analysis of the artifacts and stratigraphy is expected to provide insights into trade routes, social structures, and daily life of the inhabitants.
• The site's strategic location suggests its potential role as a regional center or a key outpost for resource management.

Analyze the contribution of such findings to our understanding of urban planning, trade networks, and social organization in ancient India.
Examine the challenges faced by archaeologists in preserving and interpreting ancient sites in modern India.

GS2.headline: India-EU FTA Negotiations See Major Breakthrough on Key Sectors
GS2.explainer:
• After years of intense negotiations, India and the European Union announced significant progress on a Free Trade Agreement (FTA), particularly in critical sectors like digital trade and sustainable development.
• The joint statement highlighted consensus on intellectual property rights and investment facilitation, paving the way for a comprehensive deal by year-end.
• This FTA aims to boost bilateral trade significantly, potentially increasing it by over 40% in five years, and enhancing strategic cooperation.
• Key sticking points like market access for automobiles and agricultural products are still under discussion but show signs of compromise.
• Experts believe the deal will diversify India's export basket and provide EU businesses greater access to the Indian market, strengthening global supply chains.

GS2.headline: Supreme Court Upholds State's Power to Levy Environmental Compensation on Polluting Industries
GS2.explainer:
• The Supreme Court, in a landmark judgment, affirmed the power of state pollution control boards to impose environmental compensation on industries violating pollution norms.
• The ruling came in a case challenging a state government's notification imposing fines based on the 'Polluter Pays Principle' and the 'Precautionary Principle'.
• The court emphasized that environmental protection is a constitutional mandate under Article 48A and 51A(g), and states have inherent power to ensure ecological balance.
• This judgment is expected to strengthen environmental governance and act as a deterrent against industrial pollution across the country.
• It clarifies the legal framework for calculating and imposing compensation, directing for transparent and scientific methodologies.

GS2.headline: National Skill Mission to Prioritize 'Green Jobs' Training for Rural Youth
GS2.explainer:

• This initiative aims to align India's workforce with the growing demands of renewable energy, sustainable agriculture, waste management, and eco-tourism sectors.
• The program will involve collaborations with industry partners, NGOs, and local self-help groups to ensure relevant and demand-driven training modules.
• The move is expected to address rural unemployment, reduce migration to urban areas, and contribute to India's climate action goals.
• Pilot projects have shown promising results in states like Rajasthan and Gujarat, leading to the nationwide expansion.

GS2.headline: India Calls for Review of Indus Water Treaty Provisions Amid Changing Hydrological Realities
GS2.explainer:
• India has reiterated its call for a review of certain provisions of the Indus Water Treaty (IWT) with Pakistan, citing changing hydrological realities and climate change impacts.
• The Ministry of External Affairs emphasized the need for a more equitable and efficient utilization of shared water resources, in light of reduced glacier melt and altered river flows.
• Pakistan, however, has maintained its stance on strict adherence to the existing treaty, which largely allocates western rivers to Pakistan and eastern rivers to India.
• India has been undertaking several hydroelectric projects on its allocated rivers, which Pakistan often views as potential violations, leading to ongoing disputes.
• The World Bank, a guarantor of the treaty, is being kept informed of the developments, as both nations seek a diplomatic resolution.

GS2.explainer:

• The framework, which came into effect earlier this year, aims to regulate the processing of personal data and protect citizens' privacy rights.
• Key challenges identified include ensuring compliance by smaller entities, clarity on cross-border data flows, and establishing the Data Protection Board effectively.
• The government reassured stakeholders of its commitment to a robust and adaptable framework, promising clarifications and amendments where necessary.
• The framework is critical for fostering trust in India's digital economy and aligning with global data governance standards.

• The review emphasized the need for greater collaboration between central and state governments, and increased investment in educational infrastructure.
• NEP 2020 aims to transform India's education system to meet the demands of the 21st century and foster holistic development.

GS3.headline: ISRO Successfully Conducts Critical Test of Reusable Launch Vehicle (RLV) Prototype
GS3.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) announced the successful completion of a crucial test flight for its Reusable Launch Vehicle (RLV) prototype, 'Pushpak'.
• The test involved a precise landing experiment, simulating conditions for future orbital re-entry and autonomous landing on a runway.
• This achievement marks a significant step towards developing a fully reusable two-stage orbital launch vehicle, aiming to drastically reduce space launch costs.
• The RLV-TD (Technology Demonstrator) project is a long-term vision of ISRO to make space access more affordable and sustainable.
• Future tests will focus on orbital flight and re-entry characteristics, integrating advanced materials and propulsion systems.

GS3.trivia: ISRO first successfully conducted the RLV-TD HEX (Hypersonic Flight Experiment) in 2016, demonstrating its controlled flight and re-entry capability.

GS3.headline: RBI's Monetary Policy Committee Expresses Concern Over Persistent Food Inflation
GS3.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India's (RBI) Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) concluded its bi-monthly meeting, maintaining the repo rate but expressing significant concern over persistent food inflation.
• While headline inflation has shown signs of moderation, volatile food prices, especially for vegetables and pulses, continue to pose a challenge to the inflation target.
• The MPC reiterated its commitment to bringing inflation within the target band of 4% (+/- 2%) while supporting growth.
• Analysts anticipate continued vigilance from the RBI, with future policy decisions heavily dependent on monsoon performance and global commodity prices.
• The committee emphasized the need for supply-side interventions by the government to complement monetary policy efforts.

GS3.headline: DRDO Unveils Advanced Indigenous Anti-Drone System for Border Security
GS3.explainer:
• The Defence Research and Development Organisation (DRDO) successfully demonstrated an advanced indigenous anti-drone system designed to counter emerging threats from unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs).
• The system incorporates both 'soft kill' (jamming, spoofing) and 'hard kill' (laser-based interception) capabilities, offering comprehensive protection against rogue drones.
• Developed in collaboration with private sector partners, it aims to enhance border security and protect critical infrastructure from drone-based attacks.
• The system features artificial intelligence for threat detection and classification, with a range of several kilometers.
• This development is a significant boost to India's 'Atmanirbhar Bharat' initiative in the defence sector, reducing reliance on foreign technology.

GS3.headline: Ministry of Urban Affairs Releases Guidelines for Mitigating Urban Heat Island Effect
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs released a comprehensive set of guidelines for urban local bodies to mitigate the 'Urban Heat Island' (UHI) effect.
• The guidelines emphasize green infrastructure development, promotion of cool roofs and pavements, and increased tree cover in urban areas.
• UHI effect leads to higher temperatures in cities compared to surrounding rural areas, impacting public health, energy consumption, and air quality.
• Cities are encouraged to develop specific action plans, integrate UHI mitigation into urban planning, and leverage smart city technologies.
• This initiative is part of India's broader climate resilience strategy, aiming to make cities more sustainable and livable.

GS3.trivia: Cities can be 1-3°C warmer than their surrounding rural areas during the day, and up to 12°C warmer at night, due to the UHI effect.

GS3.headline: Government Expands Subsidy Scheme for Agricultural Drones to Boost Productivity
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare announced an expansion of its subsidy scheme for purchasing agricultural drones, making the technology more accessible to farmers.
• The scheme provides financial assistance for buying drones and for establishing custom hiring centers for drone services in rural areas.
• Drones are being increasingly used for precision agriculture tasks such as crop health monitoring, pesticide spraying, and nutrient assessment, leading to higher efficiency and reduced input costs.
• The expansion aims to further modernize Indian agriculture, improve farm productivity, and address labor shortages.
• Training programs for farmers and rural entrepreneurs on drone operation and maintenance are also being scaled up.

GS3.headline: Government Invites Tenders for BharatNet Phase III, Aims for Pan-India Rural Connectivity
GS3.explainer:
• The Department of Telecommunications (DoT) has initiated the tender process for Phase III of the BharatNet project, aiming to connect all remaining gram panchayats and habitations.
• This phase focuses on leveraging Public-Private Partnership (PPP) models for faster deployment and sustainable operation of optical fiber networks.
• BharatNet is a flagship program to provide broadband connectivity to rural and remote areas, bridging the digital divide.
• Phase III includes provisions for upgrading existing infrastructure and incorporating future-proof technologies like 5G backhaul capabilities.
• The project is crucial for digital inclusion, e-governance, digital education, and healthcare services in rural India.

GS3.headline: India Crosses 100 GW Mark in Installed Solar Energy Capacity
GS3.explainer:
• India has achieved a significant milestone by crossing 100 GW of installed solar energy capacity, marking a rapid expansion in its renewable energy sector.
• This includes both large-scale solar parks and decentralized rooftop solar installations across the country.
• The accelerated growth is attributed to supportive government policies, declining solar panel costs, and increased private sector investment.
• This achievement brings India closer to its ambitious target of 500 GW of non-fossil fuel energy capacity by 2030.
• The push for solar energy is vital for energy security, reducing carbon emissions, and meeting international climate commitments.

GS3.headline: Editorial: The Imperative for a Robust AI Governance Framework in India
GS3.explainer:
• This editorial argues for the urgent need for a comprehensive Artificial Intelligence (AI) governance framework in India to manage its rapid adoption.
• It highlights the dual potential of AI for economic growth and societal challenges, including ethical dilemmas, job displacement, and data privacy concerns.
• The piece suggests that India, as a burgeoning digital economy, must proactively develop regulations that foster innovation while ensuring accountability and fairness.
• It calls for a multi-stakeholder approach involving government, industry, academia, and civil society in drafting AI policies.
• The editorial emphasizes learning from global best practices while tailoring solutions to India's unique social and economic context.

GS3.headline: Indian Finance Ministry Cautions Against Global Economic Headwinds, Maintains Growth Optimism
GS3.explainer:
• The Finance Ministry released its monthly economic review, highlighting continued resilience of the Indian economy but cautioning against escalating global economic headwinds.
• Factors such as geopolitical tensions, persistent inflation in major economies, and tightening global financial conditions pose risks to India's export and investment outlook.
• Despite these external challenges, the Ministry expressed optimism about India's domestic demand, robust public capital expenditure, and strong financial sector.
• Projections for India's GDP growth remain robust, albeit with a watchful approach towards global volatility.
• The report emphasized continued fiscal prudence and structural reforms to sustain long-term growth momentum.

GS3.headline: National Mission for Clean Ganga Reviews Progress, Sets New Rejuvenation Targets
GS3.explainer:
• The National Mission for Clean Ganga (NMCG) held a high-level meeting to review the progress of various projects under the Namami Gange program and set new targets for river rejuvenation.
• Focus areas include improving municipal wastewater treatment, industrial pollution abatement, and promoting biodiversity conservation along the Ganga basin.
• While significant strides have been made in infrastructure development, challenges persist in community participation and ensuring continuous flow (Aviral Dhara).
• New targets emphasize sustainable river basin management, promotion of natural farming, and protection of riverine ecosystems.
• The mission reiterated its commitment to making Ganga 'Nirmal' (clean) and 'Aviral' (free-flowing) through a holistic approach.

GS3.headline: India's Startup Ecosystem Continues Robust Growth, Focus on Deep Tech and Rural Innovation
GS3.explainer:
• India's startup ecosystem demonstrated continued robust growth in the first half of 2025, with a notable surge in funding for deep tech and rural innovation startups.
• Government initiatives like 'Startup India' and 'Fund of Funds for Startups' have played a crucial role in fostering an entrepreneurial environment.
• The focus is shifting towards sectors like Artificial Intelligence, SpaceTech, Agritech, and MedTech, attracting both domestic and international investors.
• Rural innovation is gaining traction, with startups addressing local challenges in areas like sustainable farming, craft promotion, and last-mile logistics.
• This growth is vital for job creation, economic diversification, and leveraging India's demographic dividend.
